segments.s601-g89: بدو گفت is a common word order in older Persian where the indirect object (بدو, 'to him') precedes the verb گفت ('said' — past 3rd person). بهرام is the subject; کین خود is a nominal phrase 'your/one's own grudge' with خود emphasizing possession ('own'). مباد is an optative/negative wish formula meaning 'may it not be', so the whole segment sets up a negative wish about that grudge.
segments.s601-g90: که introduces the content clause (what he is hoping to avoid). روز سه دیگر literally 'day three more' = 'for three more days' (دیگر here = 'more/remaining'). نباشیم is the negative subjunctive/optative form of بودن for 1st‑person plural 'that we be not / that we should not be'; used here as the consequence in the wish. شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy', completing the clause.
segments.s601-g91: برو is an imperative 'Go!'. آبکش appears here as a vocative (addressing a person by that name or epithet). آفرین خواند is a past action: خواند ('he/she chanted/sang/uttered') with آفرین as the exclamation/phrase being uttered. و گفت follows to introduce the next spoken admonition — گفت here functions as 'and he said'.
segments.s601-g92: که introduces the content of what was said. بیداردل is a compound adjective 'wakeful‑hearted' or 'vigilant in heart'; باش is the imperative 'be!'. و simply links two coordinated commands; با بخت جفت is a short predicative phrase 'paired with fortune' (با = 'with', بخت = 'luck/fortune', جفت = 'mate/partner'), i.e. 'may you be matched with good fortune.'
segments.s601-g93: به بازار شد is a concise narrative phrase 'he went to the market' (شد used as a past event marker). The purpose or complement follows: مشک و آلت ببرد — ببرد here is the present/subjunctive form expressing purpose 'to take/carry off' (classical Persian often uses present-subjunctive after a motion verb to indicate purpose). آلت here means 'goods/instruments/items'.
segments.s601-g94: گروگان is the direct object 'the hostage'. به + indirect object (به پرمایه مردی) marks the recipient: 'to a pramayeh man' — پرمایه here means 'substantial' or 'well‑off/wealthy'. سپرد is the past transitive 'entrusted/handed over', so the whole clause says he handed the hostage over to a wealthy man.
segments.s601-g95: خرید is here a past verb 'he bought' (or 'he purchased'). آنچ بایست is a nominalized/relative phrase 'that which was necessary' (آنچ = 'that which'; بایست = 'was necessary/ought to be'), so خرید آنچ بایست = 'he bought what was necessary'. و آمد دوان joins a second action: آمد ('he came') modified by دوان ('running/rushing'), so 'and he came running.'
segments.s601-g96: به نزدیک + person expresses motion toward proximity: 'toward/near Bahram' (به نزدیک = 'to the near place / close by'). شد + adjective (شادمان) is a common resultative 'became happy / was glad' (شد = 'became', شادمان = 'joyful').
segments.s601-g73: بشد is a narrative/perfective verb here meaning 'it happened / then (he) did', introducing the next action. لنبک is the proper name (subject) that follows the verb by poetic word order. و simply links clauses/verbs ('and'). آب is the direct object 'water', and چندی is an adverbial meaning 'for a while' modifying کشید. کشید is the main verb 'drew / pulled' (here ‘drew water’); in classical narrative it appears after its modifiers.
segments.s601-g74: خریدار is a noun 'buyer' and آبش is a possessive phrase 'his/its water' (آب + -ش). نیامد is a past negative 'did not come', and پدید is an intransitive verb 'appear / show up' describing the expected person; together they mean 'a buyer for his water did not appear.'
segments.s601-g75: غمی گشت uses غم + گشت (گشت = 'became') to mean 'he became sorrowful.' The conjunction و links the next action. پیراهنش is 'his shirt' (پیراهن + ـش possessive), and درکشید is used as 'pulled on / drew (the garment) in' — here 'he pulled his shirt on/adjusted it.'
segments.s601-g76: یکی functions as 'one' / 'one of them' or 'a certain (person)'. آبکش را is the definite object 'the colander' (را marks the object). به بر is a small locational/positional phrase ('onto/against the arm' or 'to the shoulder'), and برکشید is the verb 'lifted / raised' — so the clause means 'one (person) lifted the colander onto his arm.'
segments.s601-g77: بها is 'price' and بستد here means 'fixed / set' (classical bāstan used with prices = to settle or fix). The conjunction و links the next clause. گوشت بخرید is a past narrative 'bought meat' (بخرید here functions as perfective past), and زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the purchase.
segments.s601-g78: بیامد is a past-tense narrative 'he came'. سوی خانه means 'toward the house' (سوی = toward(s), خانه = house). چون introduces a simile 'like', and باد و دود 'wind and smoke' completes the simile — 'he came toward the house like wind and smoke', i.e., very swiftly and invisibly.
segments.s601-g79: بپخت is a narrative/perfective form meaning 'it was cooked' or 'he/they cooked' (classical b- prefix in narrative). The conjunction و links actions. بخوردند here is 'they ate' (past plural), and می‌خواستند is 'they wanted' (imperfective/past habitual sense expressing desire), indicating they desired further hospitality or continuation.
segments.s601-g80: یکی here is 'one' (a single/one) used idiomatically as 'one (occasion) / they' introducing the noun. مجلس دیگر means 'another gathering' (مجلس = gathering/session, دیگر = another), and آراستند is the past plural 'they arranged / prepared' — 'they set up another gathering.'
segments.s601-g25: This is the left part of a relative/clausal phrase introduced by the complementizer/relative که (that/who). هرکس که (whoever that) is the standard idiom meaning “whoever.” از + proper name (لنبک) marks source/possession (“from/of Lenbak”); آبکش is a noun placed after the name without an explicit ezafe (poetic omission) and functions as ‘water-dipper/water-container’ belonging to Lenbak.
segments.s601-g26: خرد آب (lit. ‘small water’) = ‘a little water’ where خرد (small) modifies آب. خوردن is the infinitive/gerund ‘to drink/consume’ used after this noun phrase. نباشدش is a negative present/subjunctive-form clause (نباشد) with the enclitic pronoun -ش ‘to him/her/it’ (referring back to the person), meaning ‘it would not be for him’ → ‘he would not be pleased’; خوش is the predicative adjective ‘pleasant/happy’. Word order places the verbal predicate at the end (poetic), so the whole means ‘it would not be pleasant for him to drink even a little water.’
segments.s601-g27: همی بود is an iterative/imperfective phrasing (‘kept being’ / ‘it continued’). تا introduces a temporal limit ‘until.’ زرد گشت آفتاب is inverted/poetic order: adjective + verb + subject — literally ‘yellow became the sun,’ i.e. ‘the sun became/yellowed.’ گشت is the past/become verb.
segments.s601-g28: نشست is the past form ‘sat down/settled’ (here probably ‘sat down’ or ‘came to rest’). از بر باره is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘from the burden/ because of the load’ (باره = load/burden). بی‌زور و تاب are predicative adjectives joined by و: بی‌زور ‘without strength/weak’ and تاب ‘without endurance/energy’; together they describe the state resulting from the burden.
segments.s601-g29: سوی + noun is the directional prepositional phrase ‘toward Lenbak’s house’ (سوی = toward, خانه لنبک = Lenbak’s house; ezafe again omitted poetically). آمد is the past ‘came.’ چو باد is a simile ‘like the wind’ (چو = as/like, باد = wind) modifying the manner of his coming.
segments.s601-g30: بزد is the simple past ‘he struck/struck at.’ حلقه بر درش is the object/locative: حلقه ‘the ring’ + بر ‘upon/on’ + درش (در + enclitic ش ‘his/its door’) = ‘the ring upon his/its door.’ آواز داد is a compound/light‑verb phrase meaning ‘he gave a cry’ → ‘he called out’ or ‘he cried.’
segments.s601-g31: که introduces reported speech/explanation (‘that’). من is the nominative pronoun ‘I.’ سرکشی‌ام uses the noun/participial stem سرکشی with the enclitic -ام; here it functions as a self‑description (‘I am a rebel / I have rebelled / I am a deserter’) — poetically compacted. ز ایران سپاه = ‘from Iran’s army’ with ز marking separation or departure.
segments.s601-g32: چو شب تیره شد is a temporal clause ‘when/as the night became dark’ (چو = when/as; شد = became). بازماندم is the past/intransitive ‘I remained behind / I was left behind.’ ز شاه uses ز to mark from/relative to whom he was left behind: ‘left behind the king’ (i.e. he lagged behind the king).
segments.s601-g81: بیود is an archaic/past form (equivalent to بود) meaning 'it was' / 'came to be.' آن شب تیره is a noun phrase 'that dark night.' با + Noun phrase (می‌به دست) means 'with ... in hand'—با is the instrument/prepositional marker and می‌به (a poetic quince‑wine) is governed by دست ('in hand'). Word order is verb → noun phrase → prepositional/instrumental phrase.
segments.s601-g82: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same,' pointing back to a previously referenced person. لنبک is a proper name, and آبکش is an appositive epithet ('the colander/strainer') placed after the name. می‌پرست is a present/habitual-form verb 'worships/adores' (mi‑ prefix marks the imperfective/present habitual); the object (what is worshipped) is understood from context (the wine/night).
segments.s601-g83: چو ... شد is a temporal subordinate ('when ... became'). شب روز شد = 'when night became day.' تیز is an adverb/adjectival modifier here meaning 'swift/quick' and precedes the verb. لنبک is the subject and برفت is the past verb 'went away/left' (classical past of رفتن).
segments.s601-g84: بیامد is a simple past 'came.' به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near' and takes the following proper name as its object: بهرام (Bahram). تفت appears after the name and behaves like an epithet or appositive; its lexical senses given ('to sauté / scorch') do not fit a name here, so its function is likely nominal/epithetic rather than the lexical verb.
segments.s601-g85: بدو is the dative/clitic-like form 'to him' (marks the indirect object). گفت here is the speech verb 'said' (the provided senses list does not include this verbal reading, so it is marked outside the given senses). روز سیم is a temporal expression ('the third day'—سیم = سوم in older/poetic usage), and شادباش is a formulaic exhortation/imperative 'be joyful' / 'greetings; be glad.' The clause is direct speech introduced by the speech verb.
segments.s601-g86: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from' (indicating separation/relief here). A series of nouns connected by و ('and') lists what one should be free from: رنج (suffering), غم (grief/sorrow), کوشش (effort/toil). آزاد باش is an imperative: آزاد (free) + باش (be!) = 'be free.'
segments.s601-g87: بزن دست با من is an idiomatic imperative 'clasp/join hands with me' (بزن used imperatively). The sequence یک امروز نیز means 'one day more / one more day also'—یک modifies امروز and نیز adds 'also/too.' Word order places the imperative phrase first followed by the time/quantity modifier.
segments.s601-g88: چنان دان is an imperative/advice 'consider/act in such a way' (دان here = 'consider/think'). که introduces the subordinate clause explaining how to consider it: بخشیده‌ای is a perfect (2nd person) 'you have given/bestowed' (used here idiomatically as 'as if you had given'), and زر و چیز = 'gold and goods/things.' The whole clause means 'treat it as though you had given gold and possessions.'
segments.s601-g65: This subordinate chunk begins with the verb phrase 'بیاریم' (a 1st-person plural hortative/subjunctive: 'let us bring' — an older/poetic variant of بیاوریم) followed by the indefinite چیزی ('something'). که introduces a relative clause ('that…'), باید expresses necessity ('ought / must'), and به جای is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in the place of / instead of'. Word order is Subject (implied) + verb ('let us bring') then the thing and the relative clause marker and the prepositional phrase that specifies its function.
segments.s601-g66: This completes the relative clause: 'یک امروز' ('one day' / 'this one day'), with با ما ('with us') marking accompaniment. به شادی is a prepositional phrase 'in / for joy', and بپای is an intransitive verb meaning 'to persist / abide' (here: 'remain with us'). The verb is in present/subjunctive/imperative colouring tied to the hortative بیاریم earlier.
segments.s601-g67: چنین گفت is a simple past reporting verb ('thus said'). The following با marks the indirect object/recipient ('to') and آبکش شهریار names the addressee ('the king's water‑bearer' / 'water‑carrier of the shah'). In poetry با can mark the person to whom something was said rather than 'with'.
segments.s601-g68: This is the content clause introduced by که ('that'): امروز ('today') is a temporal adverb, چندان functions as an adverbial intensifier here ('much' / 'to that degree'), نداریم is 1st‑person plural present negative of داشتن ('we do not have / we lack'), and کار is the noun 'work / business'. Together: 'that today we do not have much work.'
segments.s601-g69: که again introduces a subordinate clause. ناچار is an adverb/adjective meaning 'inevitable / necessarily', ز + ایدر (poetic ز = 'from' + ایدر 'here') gives 'from here', and بباید شدن is a periphrastic construction where بباید ('must/ought') + شدن (infinitive 'to become / to happen') expresses necessity of leaving or changing state ('must depart / must happen'). The order places the adverb and source phrase before the modal periphrasis.
segments.s601-g70: هم اینجا ('also here' / 'even here') emphasises locality; به نزد تو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to your presence / before you' (نزد = 'near, in the presence of'); خواهم is the 1st‑person singular future/auxiliary ('I will / I intend'), and بدن (literally 'body') here is likely the direct object meaning 'my body' or 'my person' (poetic usage). Grammatically this clause states the speaker's future action toward the addressee.
segments.s601-g71: بسی آفرین کرد is a fixed phrase: بسی ('very / much' — here an intensifier) + آفرین ('praise / applause') + کرد ('did / made') yields 'praised greatly / applauded'. لنبک is the proper name of the actor, and بروی here functions as a verbal phrase 'turned (toward)' or 'went toward' (poetic/compact ordering places the verb phrase after the name).
segments.s601-g72: ز گفتار او ('from his speech / because of his words') is a causal prepositional phrase (ز = 'from / because of'), تازه‌تر is the comparative 'fresher / renewed', کرد is a catenative verb 'made / caused', and روی is the noun 'face'. The clause means 'his face became / was made fresher by his words.' The comparative adjective precedes کرد which takes it as the complement.
segments.s601-g1: چنان ... که is an idiomatic classical construction meaning 'it so happened that' or 'so it was that'; چنان is an adverb ('so'), بد here functions as part of that idiom rather than a literal 'bad', که introduces the subordinate clause. روزی is a temporal adverb 'one day' (here placed where Modern Persian would often put it at the start of the clause). به + نخچیر marks direction or purpose 'to/for the hunt' and شیر is the direct object 'lion'.
segments.s601-g2: همی‌رفت is an imperfective past/formal past-continuous 'was going' (the prefix همی- gives the ongoing or habitual past). با introduces accompaniment 'with'. چند here means 'a few/several'. گرد in older Persian can mean 'band' or 'group' (here 'a band of men'); it follows the number word. دلیر is an adjective 'brave' modifying گرد (i.e. 'a band of brave men').
segments.s601-g3: بشد is a narrative past/occurrence verb 'it happened/there came' (classical usage = 'appeared'). پیر مردی is a noun phrase 'an old man' (pیر = old; مردی indefinite 'a man'). عصایی is an ablative/descr. phrase 'having a staff' (adjectival/epithet derived from عصا). به دست literally 'to/with hand' functions as a circumstantial phrase 'with [it] in his hand' (به + noun showing instrument/position).
segments.s601-g4: بدو گفت is a compact sequence: بدو 'to him' (dative clitic-like phrase) + گفت 'said' (simple past). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressed to someone; شاه 'king' is the addressee; یزدان‌پرست is an epithet/appositive 'God-worshipper' qualifying the king. In performance this reads as the old man speaking to the king with a vocative.
segments.s601-g5: به راهام مردیست is a locative/existential statement: به راهَـم (classical spelling راهام = 'on my road / on my way') gives the place, مردیست = 'there is a man' (existential 'is a man'). پرسیم here is an exhortative/imperative-like form 'ask (about him)' or 'you should ask'; و is the coordinating 'and' and زر 'gold' is mentioned as possession/attribute following that exhortation.
segments.s601-g6: This short noun phrase lists the man's qualities: جهودی 'a Jew' (ethnic/religious identification), فریبنده 'deceptive' as an adjectival descriptor, و 'and', بدگهر 'of evil disposition / ill-natured' (another adjective). The adjectives follow the noun in classical Persian and jointly describe him.
segments.s601-g7: به آزادگی here uses به + noun adverbially to mark 'in/for generosity' or 'by magnanimity'. لنبک آبکش is a proper name with an epithet: لنبک (personal name) followed by آبکش (epithet or byname); the noun phrase is in apposition to the preceding quality, i.e. 'a Lanbak Abkush in magnanimity.'
segments.s601-g8: به آرایش خوان و گفتار خوش continues the pattern 'in/for ...' with two coordinated qualities: آرایش خوان 'the adornment/arrangement of the table/banquet' (به + noun phrase = 'in the adorning of the feast') and گفتار خوش 'pleasant speech' (noun + adjective). The phrase lists social/ceremonial virtues (good hosting and pleasant talk).
segments.s601-g121: بیامد is a simple past third-person verb 'he came'. چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause here ('when'). In the clause گرد اسپ را زین نهاد, گرد functions adverbially ('around'/'about') modifying the placement, اسپ را (horse + object marker را) is the direct object, زین is the object 'saddle', and نهاد is the past tense of 'to put' (placed). The order (object before verb) and the use of چو + past are typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s601-g122: به indicates direction ('to/towards') and introduces the destination نخچیرگه ('hunting-ground'). رفت is the past tense 'he went'. زان here means 'from that' (i.e. 'from that house') and heads the phrase زان خانه شاد ('from that house, happy'), where شاد is an adjectival predicate describing his state on leaving; the phrase is idiomatic poetic order (source + noun + adjective).
segments.s601-g123: همی + کرد is a fossilized poetic construction expressing continuous or repeated action in the past ('kept on doing'). نخچیر (literally 'game'/'prey') with کردن functions as 'to hunt'. تا شب sets the temporal limit 'until night'. ز کوه ('from the mountain') gives the source/location; the word order places the temporal phrase before the source, which is common in older Persian verse.
segments.s601-g124: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth'. سبک modifies بازگشت and here conveys manner ('lightly' or 'nimble'); بازگشت can be read nominally ('a return') or verbally ('returned'), a flexibility common in classical Persian. از گروه marks the agent/source ('from the troop/company'), so the whole phrase means that the troop issued forth and returned in a light/quick manner.
segments.s601-g17: نماند is a third‑person singular negative verbal form meaning 'will not remain / will not be left' (classical narrative uses present/negative to express future or general fact). The prepositional phrase به فردا از امروز literally orders the temporal limits 'to/toward tomorrow, from today' (به + noun = 'until/to'; از + امروز = 'from today'), and چیز is the indefinite noun 'thing/anything' that is the theme of نماند.
segments.s601-g18: نخواهد is the negative future/auxiliary 'will not' used to introduce the following subordinate clause (که). که here introduces the content clause 'that ...'. در خانه باشد uses the present/subjunctive form باشد ('be') to say something 'be/ remain in the house'; the final به نیز ('also/too') attaches as an adverbial particle adding 'also' or 'either' to the statement.
segments.s601-g19: به is the preposition 'on/to'; راهام should be read as a possessive/nominal unit ('راه‌ام' = 'my road') — the annotation lists را but here the word functions as a noun phrase meaning 'my way'. بی‌بر is an adjective 'barren/without offspring' placed before the predicate noun, and جهودیست is the fused copular form 'جهودی‌ست' = 'is a Jew' (archaic/poetic - است attached). زفت is a derogatory adjective 'foul/nasty' placed after the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s601-g20: This is a rhetorical clause introduced by the interrogative/adversative کجا ('where' used rhetorically = 'nowhere/wherever'). زفتی functions as a noun/nominal 'filth/filthy matter' and او is the pronominal subject 'he'. نشاید is a modal/impersonal negative 'it is not fitting / cannot' and نهفت is the past participle/infinitive 'be hidden' — together 'there is no place where his filth can be hidden.'
segments.s601-g21: درم دارد is a simple present/predicate 'he has dirhams' (دارد = 'has/possesses' third person). The coordination و گنج و دینار links additional possessions 'and treasure and dinars', and the final نیز ('also') reinforces that these items are also possessed.
segments.s601-g22: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing to the kinds of goods; فرش دیبا is a noun phrase 'carpet of dība (brocade/silk)' where دیبا qualifies فرش (modifier follows the noun in Persian poetic word‑order), and و هرگونه چیز 'and every kind of thing' lists miscellaneous goods — a loose appositive expanding the inventory.
segments.s601-g23: منادیگری را contains the object (منادیگری 'heralding/announcement' marked by را as the definite object marker). بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb 'he commanded/ordered/said' (ب‑فرمود = 'he said/ordered'); شاه is the subject placed after the verb (typical Persian word order allowing the verb to precede the subject in poetic/narrative style).
segments.s601-g24: که introduces the content clause 'that/so that'. شو is the imperative/optative 'become / go and (do something)' (classical usage where شو + participle/verb gives an instruction). بانگ زن is the verb phrase from بانگ زدن 'to cry/shout' (بانگ = 'cry/sound', زن = verb 'to strike/do' used here as the verb 'make/utter a cry'), and پیش بازارگاه is the locative adverbial 'before/in front of the marketplace.' Together: 'that he go and cry out before the market.'
segments.s601-g41: فرود آمد is a simple past compound meaning 'came down / dismounted' (فرود = alighting; آمد = came). از introduces the source ('from'), and باره here means 'load / baggage' (poetic usage); بهرامشاه is the possessor 'Bahram-shah'. Word order is verb + source phrase (typical of classical Persian poetry).
segments.s601-g42: همی داشت is a past-continuous marker 'was keeping / kept' (همی = continually/still + داشت as progressive). آن باره is the direct object 'that load/baggage'; لنبک is the person (proper name) who is the agent but appears before the noun نگاه ('gaze') — poetic inversion: 'لنبک ... نگاه داشت' = 'Lankbak kept (his) gaze'. نگاه is the noun 'look/gaze' that داشت takes as complement.
segments.s601-g43: بمالید is past tense 'he rubbed / anointed' (here 'smear/anoint' is the sense). شادان is an adverbial adjective 'joyfully, glad(ly)' modifying the verb. به + چیزی indicates the thing used or applied ('with / onto something'); تنش is 'his body' (تن = body, -ش = his). The phrase thus means 'he joyfully anointed his body with something.'
segments.s601-g44: یکی here is numeric/indefinite 'one / a single'. رشته بنهاد is a past clause: بنهاد (بنهادن) = 'put/placed' (classical diction often listed as 'founded' but used here as 'placed'); بر گردنش = 'on/around his neck' (بر = on/upon, گردن = neck, -ش = his). The whole clause: 'he placed one string/thread around his neck.'
segments.s601-g45: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بنشست is past 'sat down' with بهرام as the subject. The following clause لنبک دوید is a separate simple past clause: Lankbak (subject) + دوید 'ran / hastened'. This is a typical two-clause sequence ('when X did Y, Z did ...').
segments.s601-g46: یکی is indefinite 'someone' (an unspecified person). شهره is an adjectival/attributive 'famed / renowned', modifying شطرنج 'chess (game / board)'. پیش is an adverb meaning 'before / in the presence of' (i.e., brought before the company/king). آورید is an older/narrative past form meaning 'they brought' or 'brought (there)'; classical narrative uses such forms where Modern Persian would use آوردند or آورد.
segments.s601-g47: یکی is 'someone' or 'a certain one' (indefinite subject). کاسه آورد is the past clause 'brought a bowl' (کاسه = bowl, آورد = brought). پر خوردنی is a compound attributive: پر 'full (of)' + خوردنی 'food/edibles' = 'full of edibles' — 'a bowl full of food.'
segments.s601-g48: بیاورد is another narrative/past-form verb 'brought / produced' (classical usage). هرگونه 'every kind / any sort' modifies the following noun, and آوردنی is a deverbal adjective 'that which is brought / bringable' (i.e. 'provisions of every kind' or 'every sort of thing to be brought'). Together: 'he brought every kind of thing that could be brought.'
segments.s601-g9: بپرسید is an imperative verb ('ask', 2nd-person plural/formal) placed at the clause start; زان is a poetic contraction of از آن ('from those/of that'), marking the source (i.e. 'ask of those'); کهتران is the noun 'the elders' (here the addressees) and forms the object of بپرسید; کاین کیند is a following relative clause ('who these are'), with کاین 'these/that which' + کیند a present/plural verb 'they are/do' — the relative clause describes کهتران.
segments.s601-g10: This is a separate result/participation clause headed by the preposition به 'toward/to' + گفتار 'speech/words' (the indirect object); این پیر 'this old man' identifies whose speech it is; سر بر چیند is an idiomatic sequence: سر 'head' + preposition بر 'toward/upon' + verb چیند (here 'place/turn') — together 'turn (one's) head toward', with the verb at the end as in classical Persian.
segments.s601-g11: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke' (classical placement before the indirect object here); با او 'with him' marks the interlocutor; یکی نامدار is a post‑verbal subject phrase 'a certain famed one' (یکی = 'a certain/someone', نامدار = 'renowned'), reflecting the common Persian inversion where the verb can precede the subject.
segments.s601-g12: که‌ای is a relative/identifying particle here ('who is' / 'who was'); it introduces the appositive/relative clause that follows. با + گهر نامور شهریار is a nominal phrase describing that man: با 'with' (instrumental/ornamental), گهر 'gem/jewel', نامور 'renowned', شهریار 'king' — i.e. 'a king famed for jewels' (adjectival chain modifies the antecedent).
segments.s601-g13: سقاایست is a compound/predicative form meaning 'is a s̱aqā / water‑carrier/cupbearer' (the element -است 'is' is fused); این لنبک آبکش is the subject noun phrase 'this Lenbak, the water‑carrier' (proper name + epithet). The verb/copula is incorporated into سقاایست so the clause reads 'This Lenbak the water‑carrier is a s̱aqā.'
segments.s601-g14: This is a coordinate adjectival/nominal description of the same person: جوانمرد 'chivalrous man' then و 'and' introduces additional attributes introduced by با 'with' — خوان 'table/banquet' (i.e. hospitality) and گفتار خوش 'pleasant speech'. The sequence lists qualities (he is chivalrous and provides good hospitality and pleasant speech).
segments.s601-g15: The clause begins with the preposition به 'for/to' followed by a time expression (نیم روز 'half a day' with an implied یک 'one'); آب دارد 'has water' is the core predicate (دارد = 'has'). نگاه here functions idiomatically with دارد to mean 'keeps/has in store' (not the literal 'gaze'), so the whole clause says he possesses water for half a day.
segments.s601-g16: دگر نیمه 'the other half' is the temporal counterpart; مهمان بجوید is a present-tense verb phrase 'he seeks a guest' (بجوید = 'seeks/looks for'); ز راه 'from the road' (ز = 'from', راه = 'road/way') gives the source/location — i.e. for the other half of the time he goes looking for travelers to host.
segments.s601-g113: برو is an interjection/imperative here (an exclamatory particle), آفرین is an acclamation meaning “bravo,” and کرد is the past verb “did/uttered” used with آفرین to mean “uttered/praised.” The subject بهرامشاه follows the verb (postverbal subject), a common poetic order.
segments.s601-g114: که introduces the content or purpose of his exclamation (“that / namely”), شادان و خرم are coordinated adjectives meaning “joyful and merry.” بدی here is an archaic/poetic verbal form whose exact morphological identity is uncertain in the modern lexicon (context gives an optative/benefactive sense: “may you be/may you make”), and سال و ماه is the time expression “year and month” = ‘throughout the year (and month).’
segments.s601-g115: سه روز is a preposed time phrase “three days,” اندرین خانه is a locative phrase “in this/that house,” بودیم is the past-tense first-person plural of بودن (“we were”), and شاد is the predicate adjective “happy.” Word order (time + place + verb + predicate) is typical of poetic narrative.
segments.s601-g116: که introduces the reason (“for/because”), شاهان is the plural “kings,” گیتی is the poetic word for “world,” گرفتیم is the past “we took/seized” used idiomatically with یاد to mean “we took to memory” = “we remembered,” and یاد is “memory/remembrance.”
segments.s601-g117: به جایی is an indefinite directional/locative phrase “to some place / to someone,” بگویم is first-person present/subjunctive used with future force here “I will say / I will tell,” and سخنهای تو is the possessive phrase “your words” (سخنهای = سخن‌ها + ezâfe implied).
segments.s601-g118: که marks purpose/result (“so that”), روشن شود is a subjunctive passive/mediopassive construction meaning “may be made clear / may become clear,” زو is the older/poetic form of ز او meaning “to him / from him” (here best understood as ‘to him’), and دل و رای تو = “your heart and your counsel/opinion” is what is to become clear. In short: “so that your heart and counsel may become clear to him.”
segments.s601-g119: که introduces the content (“that”), این میزبانی is the noun phrase “this hospitality/hosting,” ترا is the clitic object “you” (second-person suffix written as separate token), را is the definite/object marker emphasizing ترا, بر is a prepositional/advective particle “upon/over” here combining with دهد to mean “bestow upon,” and دهد is the present/subjunctive or historical present “gives/grants.”
segments.s601-g120: چو is a conditional/temporal conjunction “when/if,” افزون is an adjective/adverb “additional/more,” دهی is the second-person present/subjunctive/continuous of دادن (“you give”/“if you give”), تخت و افسر is a coordinated direct object “throne and helmet,” and دهد is the verb “he gives” (third-person). The clause means: if/when you add more, he gives a throne and helmet.
segments.s601-g105: به‌روز چهارم is a temporal phrase 'on the fourth day' (به‌روز + ordinal چهارم). چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal subordinate clause. بفروخت is a past-form verb used poetically/intransitively here meaning that the light/dawn 'parted/broke', with هور ('light, splendor') as the personified subject.
segments.s601-g106: شد + بیدار is an auxiliary + predicative adjective construction meaning 'became awake' = 'awoke'; از خواب 'from sleep' is the usual idiom. The proper name بهرام گور follows the verbal phrase, as is common in older poetic word order.
segments.s601-g107: بشد is a narrative particle here ('then/it happened') introducing reported speech. میزبان گفت is 'the host said' (noun + verb). کای is the vocative 'O', and نامدار is an address adjective 'renowned'.
segments.s601-g108: ببودی is a second-person past/subjunctive form used here to express a hypothetical/interrogative 'would you be' or 'are you (to be)'. درین = در + این 'in this' places the location after the verb as in archaic/poetic order; تنگ و تار are two adjectival modifiers 'narrow and dim/dark' following the noun خانه.
segments.s601-g109: بدین here functions as a fused demonstrative 'in this' (ب + دین), placing emphasis on this house. اندر 'in/within' is an older preposition reinforcing location. تن‌آسان is an adjectival phrase 'easy-bodied / fond of ease' (i.e. accustomed to comfortable living). نه‌ای is the present copula with negation 'you are not.'
segments.s601-g110: گر = 'if' introduces the conditional clause. از شاه ایران marks the source/agent ('of/from the Shah of Iran') and هراسان نه‌ای is the predicate 'you are not frightened' (هراسان = frightened; نه‌ای = are not / there is not).
segments.s601-g111: دو هفته is a duration expression 'two weeks'. بدین خانه again 'in this house' (demonstrative + noun). بی‌نوا is an adjectival description 'poor / pitiable' qualifying the house or the person remaining there.
segments.s601-g112: بباشی‌گر should be read as بباشی + گر = 'if you stay' (present subjunctive + conditional particle 'گر'). آید is 'comes' (present), دلت is 'your heart' (دل + 2nd-person suffix ت), را is the object marker, and هوا here means 'desire/longing' — together 'if your heart's desire comes'.
segments.s601-g57: شگفت آمد is an impersonal/predicative phrase meaning "it was a wonder / he was astonished"; او را is the experiencer marked with the object marker را (literally “it astonished him”); ازان introduces the cause/source "from that" and جشن اوی = "that feast of hers" (اوی is an older possessive form 'his/her'), so the whole clause means "that feast of hers amazed him."
segments.s601-g58: وزان here (= و + از آن) links another thing he marvelled at: "and at that"; خوب‌گفتار is a compound adjective meaning "eloquent/pleasant-speaking"; the second وزان repeats the link and تازه روی = "fresh-faced" (تازه modifies روی). The sequence lists parallel qualities that astonished him.
segments.s601-g59: بخفت is a past tense verb (3rd sg.) of خفتن "to sleep" = "he slept"; آن شب is the temporal phrase "that night"; و links to the next temporal phrase بامداد پگاه (a poetic doubling meaning "at dawn/early morning").
segments.s601-g60: از آواز او is a prepositional/causal phrase "at/by/from her voice" indicating what caused or accompanied the waking; چشم بگشاد شاه has the older word order (object noun + verb + subject) meaning "the king opened his eyes": بگشاد is past "opened" and شاه is the subject placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s601-g61: چنین is an adverb "thus/so"; گفت is the verb "said" (here placed before the speaker, a common poetic order); لنبک is the speaker's name and به بهرام گور is the indirect object phrase "to Bahram Gur" (گور here is the epithet 'Gūr'/'onager' attached to Bahram).
segments.s601-g62: که introduces the reported clause. شب بی‌نوا is a noun phrase "a night bereft / miserable" (بی‌نوا = wretched/misfortunate); بد is the predicative adjective "bad" and همانا is an emphatic adverb "indeed/forsooth"; ستور (steed/horse) appears at the end as the thing affected, so the literal sense is "a miserable night is, truly, bad for the steed."
segments.s601-g63: یک (one) + امروز (today) gives the time/quantity "for one day / today"; مهمان من باش is an imperative phrase "be my guest" with باش as the 2nd person imperative; وبس is a final particle meaning "and that's all / only" limiting the offer.
segments.s601-g64: وگر introduces a conditional "but if / and if"; یار خواهی is a 2nd-person present/future sense "if you desire a companion" (خواهی = "you wish/you will"); بخوانیم is 1st pl. subjunctive/optative "we will call/summon" (an offer), and کس is the indefinite object "someone."
segments.s601-g49: به + proper name + گفت is the typical way to mark the indirect object: به بهرام گفت = 'he said to Bahram.' گفت here is the past verbal form 'said.' ای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly, and گرانمایه (adjective) precedes the noun مرد in the usual Persian order, giving 'esteemed/noble man.'
segments.s601-g50: بنه مهره بازی is a compact nominal phrase: بنه (a pile / pistachio) modified by مهره بازی ('beads/game pieces' used here as the type or form of the snack); از بهر is an archaic prepositional phrase meaning 'for' or 'for the sake of' and governs the purpose phrase خورد ('eating'). In other words: 'set out pistachios (or a small heap) for eating.'
segments.s601-g51: بدید is the past tense 'saw' and begins the clause (verb-first ordering is common in older/poetic Persian). آنک is the relative/'that which' introducing the subordinate clause: 'that Kalanbak gave it to him.' بدو is the clitic pronoun 'to him' (indirect object), داد is past 'gave,' and شاه at the end is the subject of the main clause ('the king saw...').
segments.s601-g52: بخندید 'he laughed' is simple past. و is the coordinating 'and.' بنهاد is an older past form (بنهادن → نهادن) meaning 'placed' or 'set down' (the b- prefix is an archaic/perfective element). بر پیش گاه is a prepositional phrase: بر 'on/before' + پیشگاه ('presence' / 'the royal presence'), so 'placed it before the presence/court.'
segments.s601-g53: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' نان خورده شد is a result/state construction: خورده (past participle 'eaten') + شد (auxiliary 'became/was') → 'had been eaten' or 'was eaten.' میزبان در زمان then places the agent 'the host' in time/location: 'the host, at that time.'
segments.s601-g54: بیاورد is the past 'he brought.' جامی is an indefinite object 'a cup' (the suffix -ی makes it nonspecific). ز is the poetic form of از 'from/of,' and می‌شادمان is a descriptive clause attached to the wine: mi-شاد-مان is an older form meaning 'that makes us glad' (literally 'wine that rejoices us'). Thus: 'brought a cup of the wine that makes us glad.'
segments.s601-g55: همی is an archaic progressive particle meaning 'kept on' or 'was continually'; خورد is the past 'drank.' تا introduces the limit 'until,' and گشت مست is 'became drunk' (گشت = became, مست = drunk). Together: 'Bahram kept drinking until he became drunk.'
segments.s601-g56: به خوردنش is composed of به + خوردن + -ش (possessive), literally 'at/regarding his eating' or 'because of his eating.' آنگه is an adverb 'then.' بیازید is an archaic/periphrastic past form of a verb meaning roughly 'burst forth' or 'struck/clapped' and here takes دست ('hand') as the thing acted with or acted upon: idiomatically 'he clapped/struck his hand' (a gesture accompanying the revelry).
segments.s601-g33: درین is the archaic fused form of در + این ('in this'); خانه is the noun 'house'; امشب is the time adverb 'tonight' placed before the verb phrase. درنگم is درنگ (to stay/linger) + enclitic -م 'my' = 'my staying/let me stay', and دهی is the optative/subjunctive 3rd-person form of دادن used poetically as a polite request/wish 'may you give / grant'. The literal order is 'in this house tonight my-staying may-you-grant'.
segments.s601-g34: همه = 'all/everyone'; مردمی here describes people present ('of the people', i.e. common folk) used as a predicate; باشد is the optative/subjunctive 3rd-person singular 'may it be/let it be'; و is the coordinating 'and'; فرهی is an adjective 'noble, well-bred'. Together this clause expresses a wish that the assembly be composed of both ordinary folk and nobles.
segments.s601-g35: ببد is an archaic/perhaps past/stative verb meaning 'became' (rather than a simple 'was'); شاد = 'glad/happy'; لنبک is a proper name (the person who is affected); ز = poetic 'from' or 'because of'; آواز = 'voice/song' (here 'voice/song'); اوی is the old third‑person possessive/pronoun 'his'. The clause literally: 'Lanbak became glad from his voice.'
segments.s601-g36: وزان is و + زان 'and from that' linking to the previous clause; خوب‌گفتار is a compound adjective 'well-spoken/eloquent'; دمساز is a compound noun 'companion‑musician / minstrel' (literally someone who makes accompaniment); اوی again is 'his'. The phrase says that by/through that voice he became an eloquent accompanying musician (i.e., the voice made him an accomplished minstrel).
segments.s601-g37: بدو here is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto it' (classical order) and combines with گفت to mean 'he said to him'; زود = 'quickly/soon'; اندر = poetic 'into/within' used with the following command; آی‌ای is a vocative‑imperative 'O! come!' (آی + ای), and سوار is vocative 'rider/horseman'. Word order places the indirect object before the quotation/command.
segments.s601-g38: که introduces a subordinate purpose/wish clause here ('so that'); خشنود = 'pleased, content'; باد is the optative/jussive particle 'may (it be)' used to express a wish; ز + تو = 'with regard to you / because of you' (i.e. 'with you'); شهریار = 'the king' used as the subject of the optative. Together it reads 'that the king may be pleased with you.'
segments.s601-g39: اگر is the conditional 'if'; با تو literally 'with you' here functions as 'towards you / against you' (i.e. those who wrong you); ده تن = 'ten persons' (ten men); بدی is the noun 'harm/evil' or the act of doing wrong. The clause sets a hypothetical: 'If ten men do you wrong...'.
segments.s601-g40: همه یک به یک is an idiom 'all, one by one'; به/بر combine with سرم (classical ordering) to indicate 'upon my head'—a formulaic way to say 'fall upon me / be laid upon me' (i.e. I will take them on). مه here is an old enclitic form corresponding to 'مرا' (me) in many epic texts; بدی (again) is 'you will do / they will do' or 'let them fall' depending on context. The whole clause expresses the speaker's willingness that each offender come upon him in turn.
segments.s601-g97: بدو (to him) is an indirect-object/adverbial placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense 'said' (here used as the reporting verb). یاری ده is an imperative construction: یاری (a helping, 'help') + ده (imperative of دادن 'to give'), and اندر خورش is a prepositional phrase 'into the stew' that follows the imperative.
segments.s601-g98: که introduces a subordinate/casual clause 'for/because.' The clause has subject مرد 'a man,' the prepositional phrase از خورشها 'from/through stews,' the light verb کند 'does/makes' and the noun پرورش 'nourishment/upbringing' — together پرورش کردن = 'to nurture/raise' (here 'is nourished'). Word order places the agent/subject before the from-phrase and the verb at the end, typical of Persian clauses.
segments.s601-g99: ازو 'from him' is a genitive/prepositional clitic indicating source. بستد is an older/past form (from بستن) used here in a concrete sense 'bound/removed/took' (archaic usage — context shows 'they took/handled the meat'). آن گوشت بهرام is a noun phrase 'that meat of Bahram.' زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' placed after the noun phrase.
segments.s601-g100: برید is a past verb 'cut' (here understood as the action performed on the meat). The conjunction و links the clauses. بر آتش 'onto the fire' is a locative prepositional phrase; خورشها 'the stews' is the object being placed; فزود (past of فزودن) means 'added / increased' — here 'added (to the pots).' Note the object and locative appear before the verb, which is common in this poetic/classical word order.
segments.s601-g101: چو is a conjunction 'when.' نان خورده شد is a perfect/passive-like construction 'the bread was eaten' (خورده is the past participle, شد as auxiliary). می‌گرفتند is an imperfect/habitual past 'they would take' or 'they took repeatedly.' و جام continues the sentence by introducing the object 'and the cup' which the verb governs.
segments.s601-g102: نخست 'first' is an adverb modifying the following action. از شهنشاه here marks whose name it is ('of/from the Shahanshah'). بردند is the past-tense verb 'they took/they uttered' — together with نام (name) it forms the idiom نام بردن 'to take/mention a name.' The word order places the adverb and genitive phrase before the verb, with the object نام at the end.
segments.s601-g103: چو 'when.' می‌خورده شد is an archaic/periphrastic expression meaning 'it began to happen' or 'it befell' — here used with خواب 'sleep' to mean 'as sleep was coming on.' خواب را (with را as definitive/object marker) is the patient of the following verb; جای کرد (جای + کرد) literally 'made a place' i.e. 'prepared a place/lay down for sleep.'
segments.s601-g104: به بالین او is a locative phrase 'at/for his pillow / at his bedside' (به + بالین + pronominal او). شمع is the object 'a candle.' بر پای کرد is literally 'made/placed upon the foot/base' — idiomatically here 'set it by/at his bedside (pillow).' کرد is the past tense 'he made/placed.'

sentences.s601-s45: Bahram said to him, "May that grudge of yours not be — that for three more days we should not be happy."
sentences.s601-s46: He called, "Go, Ābkash, sing 'Afarin'," and said, "Be wakeful‑hearted and matched with good fortune."
sentences.s601-s47: He went to the bazaar to take musk and goods, and he entrusted the hostage to a substantial (wealthy) man.
sentences.s601-s48: He bought what was needed and came running; he drew near Bahram and became joyful.
sentences.s601-s37: Lenbak then drew water for a while; no buyer for his water appeared.
sentences.s601-s38: He grew sorrowful and pulled on his shirt; one of them lifted a colander onto his arm.
sentences.s601-s39: He fixed the price and bought the meat quickly; he came to the house like wind and smoke.
sentences.s601-s40: They cooked and ate, and they wanted more; they prepared another gathering.
sentences.s601-s13: that whoever would not find it pleasant to drink even a little water from Lenbak's water-dipper.
sentences.s601-s14: It continued until the sun grew yellow; he sat down, weary from the load and lacking strength.
sentences.s601-s15: He came toward Lenbak's house like the wind; he struck the ring on its door and called out.
sentences.s601-s16: that I, having broken away from Iran's army, was left behind the king when night grew dark.
sentences.s601-s41: That dark night came with a cup of quince-wine in hand; the same Lanbak, the strainer, adored it.
sentences.s601-s42: When night turned to day, Lanbak quickly departed and came near to Bahram (Teft/Fate).
sentences.s601-s43: He said to him, 'Be joyful on the third day; be free from suffering, sorrow, and toil.'
sentences.s601-s44: Clasp hands with me for one more day as well; consider it as if you had given gold and goods.
sentences.s601-s33: Let us bring something that ought to take the place of this one day and remain with us in joy.
sentences.s601-s34: Thus he said to the water‑bearer of the king: 'Today we do not have much work.'
sentences.s601-s35: For he must necessarily depart from here; I too will come to you here with my body.
sentences.s601-s36: Lanbak applauded greatly and turned toward him; at his words his face became fresher.
sentences.s601-s1: It so happened that one day a lion went out to hunt with a small band of brave men.
sentences.s601-s2: An old man appeared, a staff in his hand; to him (the old man) said, 'O king, worshipper of God...'
sentences.s601-s3: He said, 'There is a man on my way — ask about him; he has gold, a deceptive and evil-hearted Jew.'
sentences.s601-s4: In generosity he is Lanbak Abkush; (he is) notable for the adornment of the table and pleasant speech.
sentences.s601-s61: He came; when he put the saddle on the steed he went to the hunting-ground, leaving that house happily.
sentences.s601-s62: He hunted from the mountain until night; then the troop issued forth and returned lightly.
sentences.s601-s9: Nothing of today will remain until tomorrow; nothing will be left in the house either.
sentences.s601-s10: On my road there is a barren, foul Jew; there is no place where his filth can be hidden.
sentences.s601-s11: He has dirhams, and treasure and dinars as well; likewise brocade carpets and every kind of thing.
sentences.s601-s12: The king ordered a herald, saying: 'Go and cry out before the marketplace.'
sentences.s601-s21: He dismounted from Bahram-shah's baggage; Lankbak kept his gaze fixed on that load.
sentences.s601-s22: He happily anointed his body with something; he then placed a single thread about his neck.
sentences.s601-s23: When Bahram sat down, Lankbak ran; someone brought forward a famed chessboard.
sentences.s601-s24: Someone brought a bowl full of edibles; (he) brought every kind of thing to be brought.
sentences.s601-s5: Ask those elders who these are; turn your head toward the speech of this old man.
sentences.s601-s6: Thus spoke with him a certain renowned man, who was a king famed for his jewels.
sentences.s601-s7: This Lenbak the water‑carrier is a s̱aqā; a chivalrous man, with good table and pleasant speech.
sentences.s601-s8: He has water for half a day; for the other half he seeks a guest from the road.
sentences.s601-s57: Bahram Shah cried "Bravo!" — may you be joyful and glad through the year and month.
sentences.s601-s58: We were happy in that house for three days, for we recalled the kings of the world.
sentences.s601-s59: I will put your words before someone (or in some place), so that your heart and counsel may be made clear to him.
sentences.s601-s60: That this hospitality will bring you reward: when you give more, he will grant (you) a throne and a helmet.
sentences.s601-s53: On the fourth day, when dawn broke, Bahram Gur awoke from sleep.
sentences.s601-s54: Then the host said, 'O renowned one, would you be in this narrow, dim house?'
sentences.s601-s55: You are not one to live in ease in this house — provided you are not afraid of the Shah of Iran.
sentences.s601-s56: If you stay two weeks in this poor house, and if a longing comes to your heart...
sentences.s601-s29: That feast of hers astonished him — and at her eloquent speech and her fresh face.
sentences.s601-s30: He slept that night, and at the break of dawn the king opened his eyes at her voice.
sentences.s601-s31: Thus Lenbak said to Bahram Gūr: "A miserable night is indeed bad for the steed."
sentences.s601-s32: Be my guest for one day and that's enough; but if you want a companion, we will summon someone.
sentences.s601-s25: He said to Bahram, "O esteemed man, set out the pistachios (or small heap of nuts) for eating."
sentences.s601-s26: The king saw that Kalanbak had given it to him; he laughed and set it down before the presence (i.e., placed it before the court).
sentences.s601-s27: When the bread had been eaten, the host, at that moment, brought a cup of wine that makes us glad.
sentences.s601-s28: Bahram kept drinking until he became drunk; then, at his eating, he clapped/struck his hand.
sentences.s601-s17: Let me remain in this house tonight; may everyone here be common folk and nobles.
sentences.s601-s18: Lanbak became glad at his voice, and from that (voice) he became an eloquent, accompanying musician.
sentences.s601-s19: He said to him, 'Come in quickly, O rider, so that the king may be pleased with you.'
sentences.s601-s20: If ten men do you harm, let them all, one by one, fall upon me.
sentences.s601-s49: He said to him, "Give a helping into the stew, for a man is nourished by stews."
sentences.s601-s50: They removed that piece of meat from him; Bahram quickly cut it and added it to the stew-pots on the fire.
sentences.s601-s51: When the bread had been eaten, they would take up the cup, and first of all they mentioned the Shahanshah's name.
sentences.s601-s52: When sleep began to come, he prepared a place for it; he set a candle at his pillow.
